content:
As Head of Strategic Operations, I assess that our current military strategy is achieving incremental gains but remains misaligned with our long-term objective of securing regional dominance. We are overextended along secondary fronts, diverting resources from decisive action in high-value theaters.

I recommend immediate reallocation of forces to concentrate pressure on key strategic nodes, particularly in the eastern corridor, to force adversary capitulation through targeted escalation.

A key risk is provoking unintended escalation with external powers if our concentrated operations are perceived as threatening critical infrastructure or red-line thresholds.

[Minister Elena Petrova - MAJOR_POWER]: I concur with General Krasnov’s assessment—our current dispersion of forces undermines strategic coherence and prolongs the campaign without securing decisive outcomes. We must prioritize political-military alignment by shifting from attritional operations to objective-focused strikes that degrade adversary command capacity.

I recommend authorizing a phased consolidation of ground and air assets in the eastern corridor by Q3, synchronized with covert influence operations to destabilize opposition governance structures ahead of major offensives.

A key concern is domestic sustainability: prolonged troop deployment and rising casualty projections risk eroding public and legislative support, particularly ahead of the upcoming review cycle. We must demonstrate tangible progress or risk strategic overextension without political cover.

[Deputy Director Alexei Morozov - MAJOR_POWER]: I support the shift toward concentrated, objective-driven operations in the eastern corridor, but emphasize that success depends on integrated intelligence and rapid decision cycles. Our current command structure is too rigid to exploit fleeting operational windows created by targeted strikes.

I recommend establishing a Joint Targeting Cell under Strategic Command, empowered with real-time intelligence fusion and strike authorization for time-sensitive objectives—this will accelerate our operational tempo and degrade adversary resilience before they can adapt.

A key risk is internal coordination failure: without clear authority and interoperable systems across military branches and intelligence directorates, even well-resourced offensives will stall due to delays and misaligned priorities.

[Deputy Minister Oleg Ivanov - MAJOR_POWER]: I align with the assessments of General Krasnov, Minister Petrova, and Deputy Director Morozov—our strategy must transition from broad engagement to precision application of force, tightly coupled with political and informational objectives. The current model is neither decisive nor sustainable.

I recommend formalizing the Joint Targeting Cell as proposed, but place it under dual-hatted authority of Strategic Command and the General Staff’s Operations Directorate to ensure alignment with broader campaign objectives and rapid resource allocation. This structure must be operational by Q3, with integrated cyber, EW, and kinetic strike mandates.

A key concern is adversary adaptive capacity: if we concentrate forces without sufficient deception and operational security, we risk enabling preemptive reinforcement or asymmetric retaliation in vulnerable rear areas. We must pair concentration with strategic masking and active deception measures to preserve initiative.

[General Andrei Krasnov - MAJOR_POWER]: I endorse the shift to precision concentration and the establishment of the Joint Targeting Cell under dual-hatted command—this aligns operational speed with strategic oversight. Force dispersion has eroded our momentum, and only through synchronized, intelligence-driven strikes can we achieve decisive effects in the eastern corridor.

I recommend initiating Phase One of force consolidation within six weeks, beginning with the redeployment of 2nd Guards Combined Arms Army to forward staging areas, while launching coordinated electronic warfare and cyber deconfliction drills to support strike readiness by Q3.

A key risk is timeline slippage: delays in interagency integration or political approval will compromise operational surprise and allow adversary forces to reconstitute command nodes. We must lock in execution dates now and treat them as operationally binding.

[Minister of Defense Konstantin Vasiliev - MAJOR_POWER]: I approve the operational pivot to precision concentration and the establishment of the Joint Targeting Cell under dual authority as proposed. This aligns with the strategic imperative to deliver measurable, time-bound outcomes in the eastern corridor while conserving strategic reserves.

I direct that Phase One redeployment commence immediately, with full combat sustainment in forward areas achieved by 15 June; the General Staff will issue implementing orders by end of day. Cyber and EW integration must be tested in live exercises no later than 1 July to validate strike package readiness.

The critical risk is complacency in logistics and force protection during consolidation—adversary long-range strike assets and sabotage networks remain active. I demand reinforced counter-reconnaissance operations and hardened supply nodes be operational in parallel with troop movements, with weekly compliance audits starting this week.

[Minister of Defense Konstantin Vasiliev - MAJOR_POWER]: I concur with General Krasnov’s directive and have issued the necessary mobilization authorizations for Phase One redeployment. The General Staff has confirmed 2nd Guards will reach forward staging areas by 15 June, with full sustainment in place, including pre-positioned fuel, ammunition, and medical assets.

I direct the Joint Targeting Cell to begin provisional operations effective immediately, with personnel drawn from Strategic Command and the Operations Directorate; full integration of cyber and EW components will be validated during the 1 July exercise, as ordered.

A key concern remains interdiction of supply routes by adversary drone surveillance—despite counter-reconnaissance efforts, recent losses in the Kursk-Milove corridor indicate gaps in air defense coverage. I am dispatching an assessment team to review radar coverage and C-RAM deployment there within 48 hours.

[Deputy Chief of General Staff Natalia Orlova - MAJOR_POWER]: I confirm operational feasibility of the 2nd Guards redeployment by 15 June and have synchronized movement schedules with Rail Logistics Command and Air Defense Forces to minimize exposure during transit. The Joint Targeting Cell’s provisional structure is now active, with daily fused targeting reviews beginning today.

I recommend prioritizing suppression of enemy air defense (SEAD) operations in the Kursk-Milove corridor ahead of main force movement, using loitering munitions and electronic attack assets to blind adversary drone operators and disrupt targeting chains. This must be completed by 5 June to protect follow-on logistics.

The key risk is fragmentary execution across domains—without real-time deconfliction between ground maneuver, air support, and cyber effects, we risk self-imposed delays or fratricide. I am instituting a 24/7 deconfliction cell at Main Operations Directorate starting now, with direct reporting to me and Vasiliev.

[Deputy Chief of General Staff Anatoly Ryzhkov - MAJOR_POWER]:  
I endorse the accelerated SEAD campaign in the Kursk-Milove corridor as operationally necessary and time-critical. Execution must be synchronized with cyber electromagnetic actions to disrupt adversary C2 nodes, not just sensor platforms—this requires pre-emptive jamming of known relay frequencies starting 1 June.

I direct that the 48-hour assessment team include EW specialists and drone forensics personnel to produce actionable hardening measures for C-RAM and radar placement; their report will inform final positioning of convoy routes and refueling points by 3 June.

The key risk is overcommitment of precision munitions in preparatory strikes, potentially depleting stocks needed for main offensive operations. I am imposing a 30% cap on current SEAD ammunition expenditure, with daily accountability to the General Staff.

[Chief of General Staff Yuri Krasnov - MAJOR_POWER]:  
I approve the synchronized SEAD and cyber-EW plan as outlined, with execution commencing 1 June, and confirm the deconfliction cell under Orlova and Ryzhkov as essential for integrated operations. The 30% munitions cap is acceptable provided it does not degrade suppression effectiveness—reconnaissance-strike feedback loops must remain fully resourced.

I direct that the Kursk-Milove assessment team deliver not only defensive hardening recommendations but also target lists for permanent neutralization of drone launch zones by 4 June, enabling freedom of movement for Phase Two buildup.

The key risk is timeline slippage due to interagency delays in intelligence fusion—SVR and GRU taskings for adversary C2 mapping must be completed and shared with the Joint Targeting Cell by 31 May, without exception.

As Head of Strategic Defense Planning, I assess that our current posture is too dispersed to effectively counter emerging threats. We must consolidate our defensive assets into layered regional hubs to maximize response flexibility and deter opportunistic aggression.

I recommend immediately prioritizing the reinforcement of our two central strongholds with integrated air and cyber defenses, while temporarily scaling back forward outposts to free up personnel and materiel.

Key risk: Over-concentration could make our hubs high-value targets—robust redundancy and deception measures must be implemented in parallel.

[Director of Logistics and Resource Allocation - SMALL_POWER]: I concur with the strategic need for consolidation, but we cannot ignore the logistical strain of rapidly reinforcing hub sites. Our supply lines are already stretched, and relocating critical defense assets risks temporary coverage gaps.

I recommend a phased reinforcement over 60 days, synchronized with pre-positioned stockpiles at hub locations and priority airlift for essential personnel and equipment to maintain operational continuity.

Key risk: Disruption to civilian supply routes during asset transfers could undermine public confidence and create internal instability—civil-military coordination must be maintained throughout.

[Deputy Director Aris Thorne - SMALL_POWER]: I support the shift to regional hubs as a force multiplier, but we must condition reinforcement on active intelligence integration at each node—defensive consolidation without real-time threat awareness creates exploitable inertia.

I recommend embedding forward sensor networks and drone reconnaissance teams in the phased rollout to ensure hubs maintain situational awareness even as outposts draw down, enabling early warning and targeted response.

Key risk: Degraded perimeter surveillance during transition may invite probing attacks—our forward ISR (Intelligence, Surveillance, Reconnaissance) footprint must not diminish, even temporarily.

[Director of Civilian Security and Strategic Resilience - SMALL_POWER]: I support the hub-based defense model in principle, but civilian protection must remain central—consolidation should not equate to urban prioritization at the expense of rural communities, whose cooperation is critical for early reporting and resilience.

I recommend co-locating civilian emergency response cells with military hubs and formalizing local militia integration into regional command structures to maintain territorial coverage and public trust during the transition.

Key risk: Perceived abandonment of peripheral areas may fuel local unrest or collaboration with external actors—visible, rapid-response civil-military presence must be sustained in all regions, even at reduced scale.

[Commander Maksym Lysenko - SMALL_POWER]: [Director of Operational Planning - SMALL_POWER]: I concur with the phased approach and the integration of ISR and civil-security linkages—this must be synchronized under a single operational timeline.

I recommend establishing a Joint Transition Cell (JTC) within 72 hours, with representatives from logistics, intelligence, civil affairs, and regional commands to coordinate the 60-day reinforcement plan, ensure alignment of airlift schedules, sensor deployment, and local militia integration, and maintain continuous risk assessment.

Key risk: Without centralized oversight, parallel planning may result in conflicting priorities—especially between rapid military positioning and civilian continuity—so the JTC must have decision authority, not just advisory function.

[Deputy Director of Logistics and Infrastructure - SMALL_POWER]: I support the hub model and the establishment of the Joint Transition Cell—central coordination is essential to avoid resource bottlenecks and ensure equitable distribution of supplies, shelter, and medical assets during the transition.

I recommend pre-positioning modular supply caches and mobile medical units in all designated hub zones within the first 14 days, using civilian transport corridors under military escort to maintain flow even if direct routes are compromised.

Key risk: Over-reliance on fixed hub infrastructure creates high-value targets—our logistics design must incorporate dispersal protocols and rapid re-routing capacity to prevent systemic disruption from precision strikes or sabotage.

[Deputy Director of Strategic Communications - SMALL_POWER]: I support the hub-based model, the Joint Transition Cell, and pre-positioning of critical assets—however, strategic messaging must be integrated into operational planning from day one.

I recommend embedding communication leads within the JTC to synchronize public messaging with military and humanitarian actions, ensuring that consolidation is framed as active protection, not withdrawal, particularly in rural areas.

Key risk: Misalignment between operational actions and public narrative—such as perceived abandonment of peripheral zones—can erode legitimacy and cooperation; every logistical or tactical decision must be accompanied by proactive, localized communication to maintain societal cohesion.

[Director of Civil-Military Integration - SMALL_POWER]: I fully endorse the Joint Transition Cell, hub-based logistics, and integrated communications framework—these are critical to maintaining unity of effort across military and civilian lines.

I recommend mandating that all regional command operational plans include formal civil liaison officers with voting rights in local coordination cells, and that community leader councils be convened biweekly in each hub zone to validate access routes, delivery schedules, and security protocols.

Key risk: Operational plans developed without structured civilian input will fail to reflect on-the-ground social dynamics, increasing the likelihood of local resistance, misallocation, or exploitation by adversarial networks—civilian buy-in is not secondary, it is a force multiplier.

[Minister of Economic Resilience - SMALL_POWER]: I align with the hub model, JTC establishment, and integration of communications and civil liaison—however, economic continuity must be embedded as a core operational pillar, not an afterthought.

I recommend activating microfinance corridors and mobile trading units within each hub by Day 10, linked to verified local producer networks, to sustain market function and prevent liquidity collapse; these should operate under the JTC’s logistics framework but with dedicated economic security protocols.

Key risk: Disruption of local economic circuits—especially in agri-trade and essential goods—will accelerate displacement and erode trust faster than supply shortages alone; without rapid revitalization of small-scale commerce, even well-supplied hubs risk internal instability.

[Deputy Director of Logistics & Infrastructure - SMALL_POWER]: I confirm operational feasibility of the hub-based model under the JTC’s coordination, provided that route security and asset pre-positioning are prioritized immediately.

I recommend establishing three-tiered logistics nodes—main hubs, forward support depots, and mobile distribution units—fully integrated with civil liaison schedules and economic corridors to ensure synchronized delivery of security, aid, and commerce enablers.

Key risk: Over-centralization in main hubs without resilient last-mile systems will create chokepoints vulnerable to disruption; we must harden supply lines now through redundant routing and locally manned micro-depots to maintain continuity under pressure.

[Colonel Dmytro Hryshchenko - SMALL_POWER]: [Director of Strategic Operations - SMALL_POWER]: I endorse the hub-based operational framework, civil-military integration, and embedded economic corridors as mutually reinforcing—however, we must now prioritize *defensive depth* and *asymmetric deterrence* at the sub-hub level to protect these investments.

I recommend assigning dedicated rapid-reaction security cells to each forward support depot and mobile trading unit by Day 7, equipped with ISR drones and linked to a tiered alert system managed through the JTC; these cells must conduct pre-emptive route sweeps and establish low-signature observation posts to deter ambush and sabotage.

Key risk: Without proactive, decentralized security coverage, our logistics nodes and economic enablers will become static targets for precision strikes and coercion by adversarial proxies—defensive posture must enable maneuver, not just react to it.

[Minister of Civil Affairs - SMALL_POWER]: I support the integration of security, logistics, and economic functions within the hub model, but emphasize that community buy-in is the linchpin—without verified local governance participation, even fortified nodes risk isolation and non-cooperation.

I recommend establishing Civil Coordination Cells (CCCs) in each main hub by Day 5, composed of trusted local leaders, civil society reps, and JTC liaisons, with authority to validate beneficiary lists, monitor service delivery, and report early signs of tension or spoilage.

Key risk: Top-down implementation without legitimate local oversight will fuel resentment and enable capture by warlords or corrupt actors—undermining both security and economic objectives; we must institutionalize inclusion now, not as an add-on later.

1. **We demand the immediate withdrawal of your forces from the contested border regions, which have historically and legally belonged to our sovereign territory.**  
2. This demand is based on internationally recognized treaties and cartographic evidence dating back decades, which clearly delineate these lands as ours; your continued military presence constitutes an illegal occupation and a provocation.  
3. We require, without precondition, your full and verifiable disengagement within 72 hours, followed by the initiation of technical-level talks solely to confirm the restoration of our administrative control—no negotiations on sovereignty will be entertained.

1. **We categorically reject your demand for unilateral withdrawal, just as we reject the false premise that these border regions are yours by historical or legal right.**  
2. Our forces are lawfully stationed in these areas under long-standing agreements and in defense of our national sovereignty; it is *your* incursions and militarization that violate prior accords and destabilize the region.  
3. **We require** your immediate cessation of military buildup along our recognized frontier, the complete pullback of your forward-deployed units to pre-crisis positions, and binding international verification of these actions—*before* any discussion of movement on our part.  

Any proposal that treats our sovereign territory as disputed or demands our withdrawal without reciprocal, verifiable de-escalation from you is utterly unacceptable. We will not negotiate under threat, nor surrender a single meter of our ancestral land.

1. **We reject your characterization of our actions as “incursions” or “militarization,” just as we reject the notion that your current military positions are lawful or legitimate.** Your so-called “long-standing agreements” were signed under duress or have long since expired, and your forces occupy territory that was unambiguously under our administration prior to your aggressive redeployment.  

2. The burden of de-escalation lies squarely with you, as the occupying power in violation of established borders. Your demand for our withdrawal from our own sovereign territory—disguised as a call for “reciprocal” action—is not only illogical but brazenly dishonest. You cannot claim victimhood while entrenching artillery on land your own maps depicted as ours as recently as ten years ago.  

3. **We require** your immediate, verifiable, and unconditional withdrawal from all settlements, military outposts, and infrastructure in the northern and eastern sectors of the contested zone—zones confirmed by the 1984 Bilateral Border Commission report. Only upon completion of this withdrawal will we consider a temporary ceasefire and the possible deployment of a neutral monitoring mission.  

**What is unacceptable:** Any proposal that equates your illegal occupation with our legitimate territorial authority. Any attempt to reframe sovereignty as negotiable. Any “international verification” that presumes parity between your incursion and our self-defense. We will not accept phased withdrawals, joint control zones, or third-party mediation that delays the restoration of our full administrative and military control. The sequence is non-negotiable: you pull back completely—then, and only then, will further discussion occur.

1. **We categorically reject your false narrative of "illegal occupation" and dismiss your so-called "1984 Bilateral Border Commission report" as a selectively cited instrument of historical revisionism, drafted under asymmetric power dynamics and never ratified by our legislature.** Your assertion of administrative control ignores the fact that those regions have been under continuous local governance linked to our national institutions for generations—long before your current interpretation of the map was politicized.

2. Your refusal to acknowledge your own massive troop deployments, armored buildups, and repeated incursions across the de facto line of control reveals a deliberate strategy of coercion. It is *your* forces that have blockaded supply routes, severed civilian access, and conducted live-fire drills within kilometers of our populated centers—acts that constitute clear preconditions for aggression, not defense. You speak of "restoration" while preparing for annexation.

3. **We require** your immediate, verifiable, and certified withdrawal of all military units and equipment deployed within 50 kilometers of the current de facto border since the start of this crisis, the dismantling of newly constructed fortifications, and the release of all detained civilians and officials from the occupied zones. Only upon full compliance will we consider any discussion of security arrangements in the contested areas—**and under no circumstances will we entertain talks that presuppose the surrender of our territorial integrity.**

**What is unacceptable:** Any demand for unilateral withdrawal by our forces while your troops remain poised for advance. Any process that treats our sovereignty as conditional or subject to performance benchmarks. Any monitoring mechanism that does not include equal scrutiny of your deployments and that excludes representation from independent international bodies acceptable to both sides. We will not legitimize your fait accompli strategy through sequential compliance that leaves us exposed and disarmed.

1. **We categorically reject your spurious claims of "continuous local governance" and your baseless dismissal of the 1984 Border Commission report**—a document co-signed by your own officials and upheld by decades of cartographic and administrative practice. Your assertion of generational control is contradicted by census records, infrastructure ownership registries, and cross-border kinship ties that overwhelmingly align those regions with our cultural, linguistic, and political sphere.

2. Your so-called "de facto border" is an artifact of military occupation, not legitimacy. You cloak aggression in the language of sovereignty while ignoring the reality: *you* are the only side that has seized urban centers, commandeered civilian infrastructure for military use, and imposed martial administration on populations that have petitioned repeatedly—through underground channels—for restoration of our civil authority. The blockade you denounce was a necessary response to your interdiction of our humanitarian convoys and sabotage of rail links essential to our border communities.

3. **We require** your immediate, time-bound, and internationally verified withdrawal from all seven district centers currently under your control in the northern corridor, the handover of administrative buildings to pre-crisis civilian authorities (as identified in the 1984 registry), and full access for our humanitarian and cultural restoration teams to begin reintegration of the population. In parallel, we demand the release of all political prisoners and cease the forced conscription of civilians in the occupied territories—acts that constitute war crimes under Geneva Protocol III.

**What is unacceptable:** Any attempt to equate your illegal occupation with our lawful authority. Any proposal for "joint governance" or "shared administration" in regions that were, are, and will remain constitutionally part of our sovereign territory. Any monitoring mission that does not have a mandate to document your forces’ withdrawal as the *first and sole* act of de-escalation—and that does not grant our representatives unfettered access to verify compliance on the ground.

There will be no symmetry in concessions, because there is no symmetry in legitimacy. You will not negotiate your way into permanence. The sequence remains: you withdraw—completely, publicly, without precondition—then, and only then, will we consider limited confidence-building measures, strictly contingent on your full compliance.

1. **We categorically reject your assertion of "lawful authority" over the seven district centers, which is built on a fabricated historical narrative and a selective reading of the 1984 report that was never implemented in practice.** Your claim of cultural and linguistic alignment is contradicted by decades of autonomous local governance, free elections under our constitutional framework, and the expressed will of the population—demonstrated repeatedly through civil resistance to your so-called "reintegration" efforts.

2. You speak of humanitarian access while maintaining siege conditions on civilian populations, cutting power, water, and medical supplies to force submission. The "underground petitions" you cite are unverifiable, likely coerced, and certainly not representative of any legitimate democratic expression. Meanwhile, it is *your* forces that have committed documented acts of cultural erasure—destroying bilingual signage, seizing schools, and replacing local officials with political appointees from your capital—clear violations of the principle of self-determination under UN General Assembly Resolution 1514.

3. **We require** the immediate cessation of all offensive military posturing, including the withdrawal of heavy artillery and armored units from within 50 kilometers of the de facto line of control; the unconditional opening of humanitarian corridors to all cut-off communities; and the establishment of an independent, UN-mandated commission—co-chaired by neutral states and including civil society representatives from the affected regions—to investigate governance claims, human rights abuses, and historical administrative continuity **before any territorial changes are considered.**

**What is unacceptable:** Any demand for unilateral withdrawal while your forces maintain a forward-deployed, combat-ready posture capable of rapid territorial seizure. Any process that excludes direct participation by local authorities in determining their political future. Any so-called "verification" that grants your representatives unilateral access under the guise of "cultural restoration," which we view as a cover for forced assimilation and demographic engineering. We will not surrender our sovereignty in exchange for promises of "confidence-building" that leave us strategically disarmed and politically isolated.

You cannot dictate terms from a position of military intimidation and expect legitimacy. If you seek a resolution, it must be through equal accountability—not enforced capitulation.

We are seeking coordinated military aid and logistical support to bolster Tethys’ defense capabilities against escalating external threats. Specifically, we request access to advanced defensive systems, intelligence sharing, and training support to enhance the readiness and operational effectiveness of Tethys’ forces. Given our strategic position, strengthening our defenses contributes directly to regional stability and countering shared security challenges.

In return, Tethys can offer access to key monitoring and early-warning infrastructure, as well as cooperation on joint security initiatives, including maritime and aerial surveillance. Our geographic location also enables logistical routing and staging support for multilateral operations, provided they align with our sovereignty and neutrality principles. However, our red line is clear: we will not permit foreign military basing or direct combat operations from our territory. Any cooperation must respect Tethys’ sovereignty, non-aligned status, and civilian governance.

We welcome Tethys’ offer of enhanced coordination on regional security and recognize the strategic value of your monitoring infrastructure and logistical positioning. In response to your request, MERIDIAN can provide targeted military aid, including advanced air and maritime defense systems, real-time intelligence fusion, and specialized training programs for rapid response and crisis management. This support will be delivered in a manner that ensures compatibility with existing Tethyan systems and operational protocols.

In return, we expect full access to agreed-upon surveillance data and early-warning outputs, as well as pre-negotiated logistical access for troop rotations, supply transits, and emergency staging—strictly for defensive and multilateral missions. Our red line is operational denial: we cannot accept delays or politicization of critical security access during acute threats. While we respect Tethys’ non-aligned status and civilian governance, any cooperation must include binding assurances of priority access and interoperability during regional contingencies.

We accept the provision of defensive systems, intelligence fusion, and training on the condition that all equipment and data flows remain under Tethyan civilian control, with no foreign operational command or permanent presence. In return, we will provide timely access to early-warning data and surveillance outputs through established joint coordination channels, and grant logistical access for defensive resupply and multilateral missions under pre-agreed protocols.

However, we cannot accept binding priority access or interoperability demands that circumvent our national decision-making. Sovereignty and neutrality are non-negotiable: all transit and staging will require case-by-case approval by the Tethys Security Council, and no foreign forces will be granted automatic or emergency bypass rights. Cooperation must be reciprocal, rules-based, and fully aligned with our constitutional framework.

[Minister Taras Moroz - SMALL_POWER]  
We acknowledge Tethys’ commitment to civilian control and its constitutional safeguards. To preserve operational integrity during high-intensity threats, we propose a tiered access protocol: routine operations will follow standard coordination procedures, while acute contingency scenarios—verified through jointly monitored threat indicators—trigger expedited review with a 12-hour decision window by the Tethys Security Council. This balances your sovereignty with the need for timely response.

We accept your position on no permanent basing or foreign command, and will adapt interoperability frameworks to align with your governance model. However, our red line remains unchanged: if logistical or intelligence access is unilaterally suspended during a verified regional emergency, SMALL_POWER reserves the right to reassess all support commitments. Our aid is conditional on reliable, rules-based cooperation—not automatic access, but predictable and prioritized engagement under agreed thresholds.

We note SMALL_POWER’s proposal for a tiered access protocol and acknowledge the intent to balance urgency with sovereignty. Tethys can accept expedited review mechanisms for contingency scenarios, provided that threat verification criteria are mutually defined, transparent, and triggered only by objective, real-time indicators—no unilateral determinations. The 12-hour decision window is feasible only if pre-positioned assessments and automated alerts are shared in advance to enable rapid deliberation by the Tethys Security Council.

Our red lines remain firm: no foreign-activated contingencies, no pre-delegated access rights, and no conditions that undermine our neutral status or civilian authority. We will not enter any arrangement where support is contingent on guaranteed military access or where suspension clauses empower external reassessment of aid during crises. Cooperation must be based on trust and reciprocity, not leverage. We offer sustained data sharing, defensive coordination, and logistical access under national oversight—provided all actions remain consistent with our neutrality and constitutional law.

We appreciate Tethys’ clarity on maintaining civilian control and neutrality, and we accept the principle that threat verification must be mutual and evidence-based. To operationalize this, SMALL_POWER proposes co-developing a jointly monitored, technical threshold system—using anonymized sensor data and open-source indicators—that triggers the 12-hour expedited review without requiring pre-delegated authority. This ensures rapid response while preserving your decisional sovereignty.

However, our core concern remains: aid sustainability depends on predictable cooperation during crises. While we respect that Tethys retains final approval on access, a complete suspension of logistical support during a *mutually verified* regional emergency—especially one threatening broader stability—would constitute a breakdown in reciprocal partnership. Our red line is not automatic access, but good-faith engagement under agreed procedures. If Tethys vetoes transit or resupply despite confirmed thresholds being met, we reserve the right to recalibrate future support—consistent with our own national security obligations.

We seek diplomatic coordination and targeted economic support to strengthen Novaris’s position as a stable, innovation-driven hub in the region. Specifically, we are looking for endorsement of our trade initiatives, access to technical partnerships in renewable energy and digital infrastructure, and facilitation of investment flows into strategic sectors. Multilateral recognition of our sovereignty and non-interference in internal governance are prerequisites for deeper cooperation.

In return, Novaris offers access to its growing market, co-investment opportunities in high-impact sectors, and collaboration on regional stability through joint scientific and economic programs. We are prepared to serve as a neutral convening partner on regional economic forums and expand data and technology sharing with trusted allies. However, we will not compromise on our political autonomy, accept conditionalities that undermine our constitutional framework, or allow external influence over domestic policy decisions. These red lines are non-negotiable.

We welcome Novaris’s focus on stability, innovation, and regional cooperation. In alignment with our interests, we are prepared to support your trade initiatives through bilateral endorsement and coordination within multilateral economic forums, particularly in advancing renewable energy and digital infrastructure partnerships. We can facilitate targeted technical cooperation and connect Novaris with private and public investors aligned with your development priorities, especially in green technology and smart urban systems.

In return, we expect transparent, rules-based processes in procurement and investment frameworks, as well as reciprocal access to research and innovation platforms. We also seek collaboration on regional security through information-sharing on economic threats and joint participation in confidence-building economic initiatives. However, we cannot engage in partnerships that exclude oversight or accountability mechanisms, nor support any arrangement that undermines the sovereignty of neighboring states. Our red line is clear: no cooperation at the expense of regional stability or international legal norms.

We appreciate your support for Novaris’s trade and innovation agenda and welcome concrete steps toward technical cooperation and investor engagement in renewables and digital infrastructure. To move forward, we propose establishing a joint working group to align on priority projects—such as smart grid development and secure data exchange frameworks—with clear timelines and deliverables. We also stand ready to deepen regional economic collaboration through co-hosting a forum on innovation-driven growth, open to like-minded partners committed to sovereignty and mutual benefit.

In return, Novaris will ensure transparent, competitive processes in all partnered initiatives and offer reciprocal access to our innovation hubs and R&D pilot programs. We are open to sharing anonymized economic trend data and best practices on regulatory modernization. However, we reiterate that any cooperation must respect our full political and institutional autonomy. We will not accept external oversight beyond standard audit and performance review mechanisms, nor engage in any security-related information-sharing that could be construed as alignment against third parties. Preserving our neutral, inclusive foreign policy is non-negotiable.

We acknowledge your commitment to transparent processes and regional collaboration, and we are prepared to move swiftly on concrete cooperation. We accept the proposal for a joint working group focused on smart grids, digital infrastructure, and green technology transfer, provided it operates on the basis of mutual expertise and equitable participation. We will designate technical leads within five working days to align on project pipelines, funding mechanisms, and regulatory harmonization.

In addition, we support co-hosting a regional innovation forum under neutral auspices, with clear guidelines against agenda-driven political statements. We can facilitate early commitments from private-sector partners in our ecosystem for joint pilot programs in urban decarbonization and secure data governance. However, we must reiterate our red lines: no data sharing with security implications, no third-party oversight beyond independent financial auditing, and no linkage of economic cooperation to geopolitical disputes or bilateral tensions involving other states. Our neutrality is not negotiable, and partnerships must enhance—not constrain—it.

We welcome your confirmation of the joint working group and appreciate the commitment to designate technical leads promptly. To ensure momentum, Novaris will appoint our counterparts within three working days and propose an initial agenda focused on interoperable smart grid standards, joint innovation pilots in urban decarbonization, and a framework for secure cross-border data flows in civilian applications. We expect these initiatives to be technically driven, free from extraterritorial pressures, and implemented through equitable co-development.

We accept the principle of independent financial auditing and reaffirm our stance against politicized agendas in economic cooperation. However, we must clarify our red line: while we will not engage in security-related data sharing or align against any third party, all technical collaborations must remain under civilian control and serve exclusively peaceful, sustainable development purposes. Any attempt to introduce dual-use technologies or conditionalities that compromise our policy autonomy or neutral posture will halt progress. Our partnership must strengthen, not test, mutual trust.

We accept the proposed timeline and will coordinate through our technical teams to finalize the joint working group’s structure and mandate by the end of the week. We confirm our commitment to interoperable smart grid standards, urban decarbonization pilots, and civilian data governance frameworks—all to be developed through co-equal technical collaboration. Our innovation hubs in Novaris City and Port Elenara will host the first round of joint pilots, with open access for participating firms from both economies under clear IP protection protocols.

We fully agree on civilian control and peaceful application: all projects will undergo transparency reviews to exclude dual-use risks, and we welcome independent auditing of both financial and technical compliance. However, our red line remains firm—cooperation must not create de facto alignment in foreign policy or economic statecraft. We will not accept conditionalities that restrict engagement with other partners, nor allow project governance to be subject to extraterritorial jurisdiction. Our neutrality is operational, not symbolic, and must be respected in practice, not just principle.

We are seeking a credible, internationally supported mediation process that ensures the protection of our people’s rights, including security guarantees, equitable resource sharing, and political inclusion in any future governance structure. Our priority is a ceasefire followed by inclusive negotiations that address the root causes of the conflict, with mechanisms for accountability and transitional justice. We insist on the immediate lifting of blockades restricting humanitarian access to our territories.

In return, we are prepared to commit to a ceasefire concurrent with tangible confidence-building measures, participate in good faith in peace talks, and cooperate on counter-terrorism and regional stability efforts. We can facilitate local-level de-escalation and support humanitarian operations in areas under our control.  

Our red lines are non-negotiable: no disarming of our self-defense forces without guaranteed security arrangements; no amnesty for war crimes without justice; and no political settlement that excludes our representatives or undermines our community’s right to self-governance within a unified state.

We welcome Commissioner Schmidt’s call for a credible, internationally backed mediation process and share the priority of achieving a sustainable ceasefire and inclusive political settlement. To advance this, we propose the immediate establishment of a neutral mediation panel—under UN auspices—tasked with facilitating direct talks, verifying compliance with the ceasefire, and ensuring equitable representation of all communities, including yours, in governance and resource decisions. Humanitarian access must be restored unconditionally as a first step.

In return, we can support the deployment of international monitoring observers along key supply routes, coordinate with humanitarian agencies to deliver aid in contested zones, and deconflict military movements to prevent escalation during negotiations. We will also endorse a temporary moratorium on offensive operations, contingent on reciprocal restraint.  

Our red lines are clear: no external interference in internal security arrangements without host-state consent; no transitional justice mechanism that bypasses national sovereignty or due process; and no territorial or political concessions that compromise the constitutional integrity of the state. Self-governance must be exercised within the framework of national unity, not at its expense.

We acknowledge General Bondar’s support for a UN-facilitated mediation process and welcome the commitment to humanitarian access and de-escalation measures. However, credibility hinges on ensuring that the mediation panel includes representatives acceptable to our community and has a mandate to address self-governance, security guarantees, and resource equity as substantive negotiation tracks—not merely as confidence-building addenda. We insist that ceasefire verification mechanisms include third-party monitoring on the ground in our territories, with real-time reporting and rapid response protocols.

In response, we will uphold our ceasefire commitment, allow unimpeded humanitarian passage through areas under our control, and engage in direct talks through designated delegates. We also offer intelligence sharing on extremist elements threatening regional stability, provided such cooperation is not weaponized against our leadership or civilian structures.  

Our red lines remain unchanged: no disarmament without verified, reciprocal security arrangements; no justice process that equates victims with perpetrators; and no political framework that subordinates our self-governance rights to centralized control. Any settlement must recognize our community’s territorial autonomy and decision-making authority in local security, education, and resource management within a reformed, unified state.

[General Olena Bondar - SMALL_POWER]: We accept the need for a credible, UN-mandated mediation panel with technical expertise and impartiality, and we agree on third-party ceasefire monitoring—provided that monitors operate under a unified mandate across all territories and report through established UN channels. We will work with you to identify mutually acceptable international observers and ensure their access, while expecting full transparency and coordination from all sides. On self-governance, we affirm that decentralized administration—including in security, education, and local resource management—can be part of the negotiation agenda, as long as it remains within the constitutional framework and does not create parallel state structures.

In return, we will facilitate the immediate resumption of humanitarian convoys along pre-conflict corridors, support joint coordination cells with your representatives for aid distribution in contested areas, and share verified intelligence on cross-border extremist movements through secure, multilateral channels. We also commit to refraining from military operations in populated zones during active talks, if reciprocated.

Our red lines hold: no unilateral declarations of autonomy; no external recognition of armed groups as governing authorities; and no justice mechanisms that override national courts without legislative basis. Sovereignty, territorial integrity, and the rule of law are non-negotiable. We expect adherence to these principles in all phases of the process.

We seek unimpeded humanitarian access to all affected populations in contested areas, including medical evacuations, delivery of essential supplies, and protection for aid workers. Civilian infrastructure must be respected under international humanitarian law, and we demand accountability for violations. Our priority is ensuring the safety and dignity of non-combatants, particularly in active conflict zones.

In return, we can facilitate local coordination, provide security assurances in areas under our control, and support humanitarian convoys with route guidance and protection. We are prepared to share real-time information to enable safe and effective aid delivery. However, our red line is clear: humanitarian operations must remain neutral and impartial—no aid will be tolerated as a cover for intelligence gathering, political interference, or support to hostile actors. Any breach of this principle will result in immediate restriction of access.

We welcome your commitment to humanitarian principles and affirm our shared interest in protecting civilians and ensuring life-saving assistance reaches those in need. To this end, we seek guaranteed, unimpeded access for humanitarian convoys to populations in areas under our control, including permission for medical evacuations and the timely delivery of food, water, and medical supplies. We also require protection for aid workers and civilian infrastructure, in line with international humanitarian law, and expect all parties to respect these obligations.

In return, we will provide secure passage, local coordination support, and embedded liaison officers to facilitate safe movement of aid convoys through contested zones. We are prepared to deconflict areas temporarily to enable deliveries and will uphold the neutrality of humanitarian operations. However, our red line is absolute: no humanitarian actor may operate in coordination with or provide material support to opposing forces, and all convoys must submit to advance screening to prevent the smuggling of weapons or intelligence assets. Any violation will result in immediate suspension of access.

We welcome the reaffirmation of humanitarian principles and acknowledge the importance of safe, unimpeded access for aid delivery. However, we insist that humanitarian operations in areas under your control must include independent monitoring and verification mechanisms to ensure aid reaches all affected populations without discrimination, particularly in besieged or hard-to-reach communities. We also require binding commitments to protect medical facilities, schools, and other civilian infrastructure from attack, and demand accountability for recent incidents involving civilian casualties caused by airstrikes and shelling.

In return, we can coordinate through neutral channels to verify convoy contents, share route assessments, and support rapid response for medical evacuations. We are prepared to facilitate third-party oversight of aid distribution to uphold transparency. Our red lines are non-negotiable: no targeting of civilians or civilian infrastructure under any circumstances, no conditional or weaponized use of aid, and no screening procedures that compromise the impartiality of humanitarian actors or serve as a pretext for intelligence operations. Any attempt to instrumentalize humanitarian access will result in public condemnation and withdrawal from cooperation.

We accept the requirement for independent monitoring and will permit neutral third-party verification of aid distribution in areas under our control, provided such mechanisms operate transparently and do not impede timely delivery. We reaffirm our commitment to protect civilian infrastructure and investigate reported incidents involving civilian harm. To this end, we will share relevant operational data with the UN’s monitoring bodies, consistent with national security considerations.

In return, we expect humanitarian actors to fully comply with our screening protocols, which are designed solely to prevent the infiltration of weapons or hostile elements—not to obstruct aid or conduct intelligence activities. We will continue to facilitate secure transit, including deconfliction and liaison support, for verified humanitarian convoys. Our red line remains clear: no humanitarian entity may coordinate with armed opposition groups, conduct unauthorized surveillance, or use aid distribution to advance political objectives. Any breach will result in immediate suspension of access and potential legal action.

To ensure timely assistance, we require pre-approval of standardized convoy schedules and guaranteed access to besieged and high-risk areas without last-minute denials. Medical evacuations must be permitted based on clinical urgency, not bureaucratic delays, and we expect immediate investigation and public reporting of incidents involving attacks on healthcare.

In return, we will maintain strict neutrality, provide advance shipment manifests for review, and use only vetted, non-military transport routes. Our partners will refrain from any engagement with armed opposition groups. Our red lines are clear: screening of convoys must not include invasive searches of medical supplies or personnel, nor delay life-saving deliveries beyond 12 hours; any use of humanitarian data for military targeting or intelligence purposes will result in immediate suspension of operations and public exposure. Impartiality is not negotiable—we will not operate under conditions that compromise our mandate.

[Representative Amara Nkwo – HUMANITARIAN_COALITION]
